text: Today's procedure is gonna be on a fifty-four-year-old gentleman who's had a long-standing ventral hernia near his umbilicus that's incarcerated. We're anticipating having to do some dissection to free up that incarceration as well as perform a repair.

The surgeon performs a primary fascial repair closure using a running V-lock non-absorbable suture, followed by the placement of an underlay mesh. The mesh is secured to the fascia around its circumference with the same type of suture for a secure repair.
The surgical team begins the ventral hernia repair by widely draping the patient's abdomen to allow ample space for port placement.
The surgical team plans a three-port approach, beginning with the insertion of a blunt tip trocar in the right subcostal area.
Under direct laparoscopic visualization, the surgeon uses tenaculums on the skin for counter traction, gently manipulating the trocar until it penetrates the peritoneum.
The surgeon insufflates carbon dioxide into the abdominal cavity, creating space for the entry of the blunt tip trocar into the peritoneum.
The surgeon allows a small amount of air to enter the peritoneal cavity, facilitating the complete insertion of the trocar under laparoscopic guidance.
The surgeon positions a twelve millimeter standard laparoscopic port, which will serve as one of the robotic arms for the procedure.
The surgeon positions the patient supine and places the laparoscopic camera port laterally, ensuring more than ten centimeters of space to prevent robotic arm clashing.
The surgeon places an additional robotic port in the lower right abdomen using a long trocar, creating space for the robotic arm attachment.
The surgeon uses a long trocar in a larger patient, ensuring ample external length for attaching the robotic arm.
The surgeon positions the bariatric or long trocars for the camera port, ensuring more than ten centimeters of space for the third port placement, where the robotic arm will be attached.
The surgeon positions the remote center of the robotic arm into the muscle layer, preparing to dock the robot for the procedure.
The surgical team directs the robot straight towards the patient and attaches the camera arm to the camera trocar, ensuring proper alignment for the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon uses a long trocar to secure the robotic arm, designating the number one robotic port site as the right-hand dissection port, which will connect to a standard 8.5 mm robotic trocar.
The surgeon inserts a vessel sealer through the trocar, utilizing a 30-degree laparoscope for visualization, which provides a view straight up at the abdominal wall.

The surgeon observes adhesions in the surgical field as robotic instruments are introduced. Arm number one, equipped with a vessel sealer, is designated for dissection, while arm number two uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument to grasp the peritoneum.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument with low grip strength in the left hand to atraumatically move bowel tissue. The bipolar function allows for effective control of minor oozing during dissection of fatty tissues.
The surgeon begins the procedure by freeing the abdominal wall from adhesions, a common step in addressing ventral and incisional hernias.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect and move the generous median umbilical ligaments in the lower abdomen, clearing the area to secure the mesh directly to the fascia without interference from fat tissue.

The surgeon uses an advanced bipolar device with a blunt tip to dissect tissue, coagulate, and cut. The device allows for cold cutting in areas with low vascularity, demonstrating its versatility in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to free the fascia, revealing the fascial defect. The goal is to close the defect primarily before placing mesh.

So with the most common laparoscopic approach to ventral hernias, is an underlay mesh technique where you simply leave the defect open, cover it with mesh, and then secure the mesh either to the fascia with clips or trans-fascial sutures and leave the defect open. More literature has been suggesting primary closure is an advantage and I think just empirically or intuitively you can imagine that closing the muscle defect is going to accomplish a more secure repair.
And we've noted some other advantages. such as a lower seroma rate early on. So I think there are definite advantages to the primary closure during a laparoscopic hernia repair.
The surgeon continues to clear the fascial edges of fatty tissue and reduces the omentum that is incarcerated within the hernia, using a vessel sealer for dissection.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to release the omentum that is incarcerated within the hernia, with occasional sharp dissection to assist in freeing the tissue that has been stuck for an extended period.

The surgeon identifies the base of the falciform ligament near the umbilical area where the hernia is located. A nine-centimeter round Paritex polyester mesh with a collagen adhesion barrier is prepared for placement.
The surgeon primarily closes the muscle defect and prepares to place an underlay mesh. The mesh is sewn into position, with options for securing it including tacking or using trans-fascial sutures.

Alright. So we won't need that back again. So I have done both removing the hernia sac and not removing the hernia sac. And removing it tends to make bleeding and seromas in the subcutaneous space. The question is whether leaving it increases your recurrence rate. And that I'm not sure of. And I don't think there's any good data on whether to take it or leave it. And we do it kind of on a case-by-case basis. But generally, I think the less dissection, the less disruption to the tissues, the better.
The surgeon strips back the peritoneum to expose the fascia, preparing the area for direct incorporation of the mesh onto the fascia.
The surgeon uses a relatively large curved needle to take a generous bite of the fascia, demonstrating the impact of needle size on the amount of fascia captured during suturing.

The surgeon uses an instrument with a tip approximately 1.5 centimeters long to take a generous bite of nearly 2 centimeters of fascia with the suture. The suturing begins inferior to the apex of the defect, ensuring the closure extends well beyond the fascial defect for secure reinforcement.

One of the advantages of the V-lock suture is it has a an eye on the tail end that we can simply put our suture through to create a our first knot so we don't have to tie a knot at this end and then at the opposite end we're going to double back the suture on itself and the friction will create our second knot so we don't need knots in this repair at all.

The surgeon ties knots in the suture to secure the repair of a larger fascial defect, starting at each end and working towards the middle. The long suture is pulled through with multiple throws to tighten the closure, allowing carbon dioxide to escape and the abdominal wall to relax, facilitating the approximation of the fascial defect.

The surgeon places several suture throws to close the fascial defect, preparing to sew in the mesh around the circumference of the defect. The use of straight stick laparoscopic instruments is visually challenging due to the variety of angles required for sewing around the 360-degree circumference.

The surgeon uses a running suture technique to tightly close the fascial defect, ensuring no gaps remain. Multiple suture passes are visible through the fascia, and the surgeon releases some carbon dioxide to relax the abdominal wall before pulling the sutures tight.

The surgeon has placed multiple suture passes through the fascia, preparing to tighten them. The suture is carefully adjusted, observing visual cues such as the bowstringing of the suture, bending of the instruments, and the fascia pulling together to assess tension.

The surgeon uses a needle holder with a scissor at the back of the jaw to cut the suture at the end of the suture line, ensuring careful handling to avoid cutting the suture prematurely while advancing the closure.

The surgeon carefully positions the suture without pulling it tight, ensuring the edges of the fascia are visible for the final throws. The suture is extended slightly beyond the apex of the defect, similar to the approach taken at the bottom, to ensure complete closure.
The surgeon parks the needle and pulls the suture tight, releasing a small amount of carbon dioxide to adjust tension. The fascia is visibly tightened as the suture line is secured.
The surgeon completes the closure of the fascial defect using a running suture. Instead of tying a knot, the suture line is backed up on itself to secure the closure.
The surgeon backs up the suture line with two or three passes, creating enough friction to lock the suture in place.
We are going to need the suture too. Let's put one in for starters. I think we're going to go all the way around probably. The two-oh, yeah. Thank you.

The surgeon parks the needle out of the way and uses a large needle holder to position a rolled Parietex polyester mesh with a collagen adhesion barrier over the fascial defect. The mesh is placed with the adhesion barrier on the inside to prevent peeling. The mesh is positioned to cover the defect, and trans-fascial sutures are used to secure it against the abdominal wall, initially held manually until the stitches secure it in place.
The surgeon uses a suture with an eye on the back to create the first knot, ensuring the mesh remains centered over the repair site, fully covering the primary suture line.

The size of the mesh has been talked about and studied and debated. Anything from centimeters of overlap to total surface area has been discussed. And then the next issue raised is what if we do a primary closure Does that require more or less or the same amount of mesh coverage as we would if we didn't cover or if we didn't close the defect primarily and all of those issues are hotly debated there's not a whole lot of You know definitive data to make those decisions and

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to sew around a complete 360-degree circle, maneuvering needle holders in various directions. The robotic system allows for dexterous needle passing between hands, facilitating efficient suturing from different angles.
The surgeon approaches the end of the current suture line and prepares to obtain another suture to continue sewing around the remaining half of the circular mesh placement.
The surgeon overlaps the suture lines by backing up and overlapping a couple of stitches, ensuring continuity without tying a knot at the end of the suture.

The surgeon overlaps the suture line at the far end, aligning it with the starting point to reduce tension. The primary suture line is visible, taking the majority of the tension from the abdominal wall, while the mesh remains centered over the repair.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 suture with a smaller needle to take full bites on the fascia, ensuring a strong repair despite the needle's smaller size compared to the one used for the fascial closure.

The surgeon crosses over the existing suture line with several throws to relieve tension, ensuring the repair is secure without a knot at the end. The primary closure is covered by a mesh underlay, sewn in with non-absorbable suture, providing complete coverage and preventing gapping between sutures.
